coredata.dc:title: Design of long-wavelength emission carbon dots for hypochlorous detection and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Jiao, Yuan
Meng, Yating
Lu, Wenjing
Gao, Yifang
Liu, Yang
Gong, Xiaojuan
Liu, Yang
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: A facile strategy for the preparation of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) with long-wavelength emission is attractively proposed in one-pot hydrothermal strategy. The resulting N, P-CDs hold exceptional optical features and display excitation wavelength-independent properties with the emission wavelength at 590 nm, which enable it with the satisfactory relative quantum yield (QY) of 15.6% in long-wavelength region. In addition, the proposed N, P-CDs demonstrates specific selectivity towards ClO− over other competitive reactive oxygen species and exhibits rapid fluorescence response time to ClO−. Moreover, the N, P-CDs exhibits low-cytotoxicity and excellent cell membrane permeability for recognizing ClO− in SMMC-7721 cells, which demonstrates their enormous potential in biological system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N
P-CDs
Long-wavelength emission
Hypochlorite
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and purification of N, P-CDs
Optical performance of N, P-CDs to ClO−
Recognition mechanism of N, P-CDs towards ClO−

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, HRTEM (inset) images and (b) the size distribution for N, P-CDs; (c) FTIR spectrum of N, P-CDs samples; (d) XPS full-survey and high-resolution XPS of (e) C1s and (f) N1s for N, P-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and the fluorescence spectra of as-synthesized N, P-CDs. The inset in top site was the pictures of N, P-CDs under the illumination of sunlight and UV light, respectively. (b) Excitation-emission matrix for N, P-CDs.
Fluorescence responses of N, P-CDs (40 μg mL−1) towards (a) 0.5 mM various cations, (b) 0.5 mM of anions and 0.1 mM of typical ROS/RNS.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of N, P-CDs solution with addition of various concentrations of ClO− (0–55.6 μM). (b) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs and the concentration of ClO−. (c, d) The linear relationship between F0–F and the concentration of ClO−.

(a) Absorbance spectrum of N, P-CDs sample (black line), ClO− aqueous solution (red line) and N, P-CDs/ClO− (blue line). (b) Fluorescence decay of N, P-CDs in the absence and presence of ClO−. The λ ex/λ em are set as 523 nm/590 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) LSCM images of N, P-CDs at the concentration of 0.2 mg mL−1 towards living SMMC7721 cells. (b–d) The ClO− with different concentration of 5 μM, 15 μM and 25 μM was introduced into SMMC7721 cells pretreated with N, P-CDs. The first panels are the bright-field images of SMMC7721 cells; the second panels are fluorescence image of SMMC7721 cells; the third panels are the overlay of the first and second panels.
Schematic of the fluorescent performance of N, P-CDs towards ClO−.

head.ce:title: Design of long-wavelength emission carbon dots for hypochlorous detection and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, And School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: And School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, And School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science and Engineering, James Cook University, Townsville, Queensland, 4811, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science and Engineering, James Cook University, Townsville, Queensland 4811, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile strategy for the preparation of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) with long-wavelength emission is attractively proposed in one-pot hydrothermal strategy. The resulting N, P-CDs hold exceptional optical features and display excitation wavelength-independent properties with the emission wavelength at 590 nm, which enable it with the satisfactory relative quantum yield (QY) of 15.6% in long-wavelength region. In addition, the proposed N, P-CDs demonstrates specific selectivity towards ClO− over other competitive reactive oxygen species and exhibits rapid fluorescence response time to ClO−. Moreover, the N, P-CDs exhibits low-cytotoxicity and excellent cell membrane permeability for recognizing ClO− in SMMC-7721 cells, which demonstrates their enormous potential in biological system.

ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorous acid/hypochlorite (HOCl/ClO−) have been extensively employed in household bleaching [1], water disinfection [2] and other manufacturing processes [3,4] due to its strong oxidation capability. Moreover, as a vital biologically reactive oxygen species (ROS), HOCl/ClO− is derived from the reaction between hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and chloride anions, which is promoted by myeloperoxidase (MPO) in activated leukocytes [5–7]. It is convinced that an appropriate amount of HOCl/ClO− can efficaciously restrain the penetration of bacteria and pathogens in the immune system. However, the excessive accumulation of HOCl/ClO− may results in all kinds of inflammation related diseases including neuron degeneration [8,9], atherosclerosis [10], osteoarthritis [11] and renal disease [12]. Thus, the development of highly sensitive and specific methods for the quantitative detection of ClO− becomes extremely critical to biological system (see Scheme 1 ).

So far, numerous alternative techniques such as chemiluminescence [13], electrochemistry [14] and chromatographic [15] methods have been employed to detection of ClO−. Increasing attentions have concentrated on the fluorescence-based sensors due to their advantageous sensitivity [16,17], superior optical feature [18,19] and cost-effective operation [20,21]. Some of fluorescent materials such as organic small molecules [22,23], metal nanoparticles [24] and semiconductor quantum dots [25] have been recognized as effective biosensors for the detection of ClO−. The inherent drawbacks of these fluorescent probes involve relatively complicated synthesis procedures [26], poor biocompatibility [27] and low photobleaching resistance [28], which severely restrict their practical applications for optical detection of ClO− in biological samples. As a newly emerging fluorescent nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn immense attention due to their fascinating advantages in terms of superior optical characteristics [29–31], favorable biocompatibility [32–34], and excellent photostability [35–37], which enable them great potential in the fields of photocatalysis [38–40], biosensing [41–43], nanomedicine [44,45] and biological labeling [46,47]. Considerable efforts have been devoted to construct nanosensors based on luminescent CDs for sensitive detection of ClO−. For instance, Ma et al. [48] developed a green fluorescent nanoprobe by covalently modifying the oPD to the surface of carbon quantum dots, which significantly improved the detection sensitivity of ClO− in environmental applications. Hu's [49] group originally designed an economic strategy adopting H2O2-assisted hydrothermal strategy for the preparation of luminescent C-dots, which exhibited multifunctional performance especially in the recognition of ClO−. Furthermore, Zhang et al. [50] constructed a facile solvothermal strategy to fabricate yellow-green fluorescent CDs and further employed it as a photoluminescent “off - on” nanosensor for the sequence detection of HOCl/ClO− and Zn2+. However, the fluorescence associated with these CDs-based nanosensors are limited to blue or green regions with excitation dependent properties, which deliver ultra-low quantum yields for long-wavelength emission luminescence and hence restrict their utilization in biological system on account of their weak penetration capability and severe photo damage to biological tissues. Thus, developing a facile method for the preparation of long-wavelength emission CDs and further constructing CDs-based fluorescent nanosensors to monitor ClO− are intensely attractive in biological system.

In this regard, we fabricated a fluorescent N, P-CDs through one-step hydrothermal technique adopting dye of Safranine T and H3PO4 as precursors. The proposed N, P-CDs exhibited orange fluorescence accompanied by excitation wavelength-independent behavior. The N, P-CDs was employed as fluorescent biosensing platform to sensitivity detect ClO−, which exhibited a fast response time to ClO− along with a lower detection limit. Additionally, the N, P-CDs also displayed narrow size distribution, superior water-solubility and favorable biocompatibility. Taking these unique characteristic, the N, P-CDs had been utilized in vitro bioimaging over different concentration of ClO−, demonstrating their promising potential for real-time detection of ClO− in biomedical fields (Scheme 1).

Safranine T was provided by Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Concentrated phosphoric acid (H3PO4) was purchased from Beijing Reagents Company (Beijing, China). Hypochlorite (NaClO) with an effective chlorine content of 10% was provided by Fuyu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tainjin, China).·OH was obtained from the reaction of Fe2+ and H2O2. 1O2 was produced by mixing of H2O2 with HClO and O2 − was derived from KO2. The ONOO− was synthesized by sodium nitrite and hydrogen peroxide under alkaline conditions. The phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solutions under different pH values were obtained by titrating a mixed solution of 0.02 M disodium hydrogen phosphate and sodium dihydrogen phosphate with 1.0 M NaOH or H3PO4. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ cm) for all experiments was acquired by a Milli-Q Plus system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 25 mg of safranine T was introduced into 20 mL of ultrapure water to sonicate for 10 min, then 1 mL of 85% H3PO4 was added. The mixed solution was transferred in 50 mL high-pressure autoclave and reacted under 200 °C for 8 h. Subsequently, the obtained CDs dispersions was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 15min) and dialyzed in ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane with MWCO of 0–500 Da. Finally, the production was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane and freeze-dried.

Typically, the fluorescence assay for the detection of ClO− was conducted in PBS solution (0.02 M, pH = 7.4) dissolving with 40 μg∙mL−1 N, P-CDs. All the fluorescence emission spectra with different concentration of ClO− were measured under the excitation wavelength of 523 nm, and the fluorescence intensities at 590 nm were collected after 1 min for quantitative analysis. Each measurement was performed in triplicate.

Human hepatocarcinomatous SMMC7721 cells were cultured with DMEM medium supplemented with 10% FBS and further incubated at 37 °C for 48 h. Subsequently, the N, P-CDs aqueous solution with the concentration of 0.2 mg∙mL−1 was introduced to hatch cells for additional 2 h at 37 °C. Choosing PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4) to wash the cells three times and further incubate with SMMC7721 cells for optical bioimaging on a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM). Moreover, the cells that hatch with N, P-CDs were further co-incubated with different concentrations of NaClO and proceed to fluorescence imaging. The signals were collected at 550–800 nm under the excitation of 515 laser.

In the early stage of the reaction, the hydrothermal temperature, reaction time and contents of H3PO4 related to the luminescence of N, P-CDs have been discussed in detail. As illustrated in Fig. S1a and Fig. S1b, the fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of N, P-CDs reaches to the optimal values under the reaction temperature of 200 °C for 8 h. In addition, the effect of H3PO4 content on the fluorescence of N, P-CDs was demonstrated in Fig. S1c, it can be found that the QY of N, P-CDs increases gradually with the introduction of H3PO4 and reaches the maximum with the amount of 1 mL.

The morphology of N, P-CDs was examined by the TEM images. As depicted in Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1b, the proposed N, P-CDs exhibit exceptional monodispersity with an average size of 2.05 ± 0.2 nm. The inset HTEM image in Fig. 1a reveals that the N, P-CDs displayed a certain crystal structure with a lattice spacing of 0.25 nm, which corresponds to the facet (100) of graphitic (sp2) carbon. The FT-IR and XPS were conducted to verify the functional groups and chemical composition of the resulting N, P-CDs. As represented in Fig. 1c, the broad absorption peak in the region of 3190 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of –OH or N–H. The peaks at 3012 and 2929 cm−1 are derived from the stretching vibrations of C–H, and the broad bands at 2407-2228 cm−1 is assigned to the P–OH bending. Furthermore, the stretching vibrations of CO, CN and PO are located at 1643, 1509 and 1035 cm−1, respectively. The XPS was introduced to verify the chemical components of the N, P-CDs (Fig. 1d). It reveals that the N, P-CDs displayed four typical peaks of C1s (282.8 eV), N1s (398.3 eV), O1s (529.3 eV) and P2p (130.3 eV). Among high-resolution spectra of the N, P-CDs in Fig. 1e, the C1s contains four chemical bonds at 284.5 eV, 285.0 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.6 eV, corresponding to the C–C/CC, C–N/C–PO, CO and COOH, respectively. The spectrum of the N1s can be divided into three characteristic peaks of pyridinic N, amino N and pyrrolic N, which mainly focus on 399.1 eV, 400.6 eV and 401.5 eV (Fig. 1f). In addition, the high-resolution peaks of O1s described in Fig. S2 mainly demonstrates three peaks at 530.9 eV (C–O), 532.0 eV (PO) and 532.5 eV (CO). The P2p peaks at 133.3 eV and 134.2 eV are consistent with P–O and P–C/P–N (Fig. S3). Combined with the results of FT-IR and XPS, it can be inferred that the N, P-CDs is composed of a π-conjugated domains in carbon core and a large number of amino and carboxyl functional groups are bound on the surface of N, P-CDs.

The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were concerned to illustrate the optical features of N, P-CDs. The sharp peak at 246 nm in Fig. 2 a corresponding to the π-π* transition of CC, while the strong peak at 277 nm is related to the n-π* of CO/CN. The fluorescence characteristic of N, P-CDs is also evaluated in Fig. 2a. It is observed that the N, P-CDs exhibits the optimal emission wavelength (λ em) at 590 nm under the excitation wavelength (λ ex) of 523 nm accompanied by bright orange fluorescence. The λex-independent performance of the resulting N, P-CDs was further demonstrated in Fig. 2b. The PL spectrum of N, P-CDs is consistently maintained at 590 nm when the excitation wavelength increasing from 300 nm to 580 nm, which is contributed to the energy band-gap affected by the surface state. Moreover, the QY of the prepared N, P-CDs in Fig. S4 was determined to be 15.6% (Rhodamine B, λ ex/λ em = 495/515–800).

The photostability tests were performed to assess the feasibility of N, P-CDs in biological systems. Compared with some commercially available organic dyes, the N, P-CDs also hold remarkable photostability under continuous illumination with a xenon arc lamp (Fig. S5). As represented by Fig. S6, the PL intensity of N, P-CDs remains stable relative to different concentration of NaCl, implying that the N, P-CDs has exceptional performance in physiological salt environment. Furthermore, the effect of pH on the fluorescence performance of N, P-CDs in the presence and absence of ClO− are further illustrated in Fig. S7. It can be found that the N, P-CDs exhibits a significant fluorescence attenuation in the pH range of 2–7 with the introduction of ClO−, which demonstrates the tremendous potential of the proposed N, P-CDs in biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical performance of N, P-CDs to ClO−
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity response of N, P-CDs towards 0.5 mM of cations (K+, Na+, Mg2+, AL3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cr6+, Fe3+, Ag+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Sn2+, Hg2+), 0.5 mM of anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, S2−, SCN−, I−, NO3 −, NO2 −, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, SO3 2−, C2O4 2−) and 0.1 mM of typical ROS/RNS (H2O2, 1O2, •O2 −, ONOO−, •OH, ClO−) were investigated in Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3b. It is observed that the N, P-CDs displays almost no fluorescence quenching effect in response to other ions and ROS/RNS except for ClO−, indicating the outstanding selectivity of N, P-CDs towards ClO− among a wide range of interfering analytes.

Subsequently, the N, P-CDs was employed as a fluorescent biosensing platform for specific detection of ClO−. With the increasing of ClO− ranging from 0 to 55.6 μM, the fluorescence of N, P-CDs (40 μg mL−1) decreases gradually and the quenching efficiency reaches about 97% (Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b). The value of △F (△F = F0 - F, where F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of the N, P-CDs in the absence and presence of ClO−, respectively) shows two linear relationship with the concentration of ClO− in the range of 0.74–5.93 μM and 5.93–25.93 μM, the corresponding linear regression equation are △F = 7.8116C-3.3815, R2 = 0.9978 (Fig. 4c) and △F = 27.5366C-133.9592, R2 = 0.9932 (Fig. 4d), where C is the concentration of ClO−. The limit of detection (LOD) of N, P-CDs towards ClO− is 46.1 nM (S/N = 3). All these results illustrate that the tremendous potential of as-synthesized N, P-CDs as fluorescent biosensors for sensitive determination of ClO−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition mechanism of N, P-CDs towards ClO−
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching mechanism of N, P-CDs towards ClO− was explored by observing the difference in absorption spectra of N, P-CDs in the absence and presence of ClO−. As described in Fig. 5 a, ClO− demonstrates characteristic absorption peak at 292 nm, which results in the decrease of absorption peaks for N, P-CDs at 278 and 516 nm along with the emergence of new peaks in 348 nm while introducing ClO− into the N, P-CDs aqueous solution. As is accepted by many researchers [51,52], this phenomenon may be ascribed to the changes in surface functional groups of N, P-CDs after binding of ClO−. More importantly, the fluorescence average lifetimes of N, P-CDs exhibits only slightly variation from 4.51 ns to 4.14 ns with the introduction of ClO− (Fig. 5b). It can be inferred that N, P-CDs undergoes a static quenching mechanism in the process of biosensing for ClO− due to the generation of complex between N, P-CDs and ClO−.

To investigate the feasibility of N, P-CDs in biological system, human hepatocarcinomatous SMMC7721 cells are chosen as criteria for evaluating the cytotoxicity of safranine T and N, P-CDs. As depicted in Fig. S8, the cell viability of N, P-CDs is higher than safranine T and reach to 80% while incubating SMMC7721 cells with N, P-CDs from 0 to 100 μg mL−1, which indicates the superior biocompatibility and ultra-low cytotoxicity of the N, P-CDs. Furthermore, the capability of the proposed N, P-CDs in biological labeling was evaluated through laser scanning confocal microscopy (Fig. 6 ). The N, P-CDs exhibits bright orange fluorescence under the excitation of 515 laser and demonstrates excitation wavelength independent properties. While introducing ClO− into the cells, the fluorescence intensity of orange channel decreases gradually and demonstrates effective diminish in terms of the concentration of ClO− (Fig. 5). Thus, the proposed N, P-CDs can be regarded as a potential bioimaging reagent for visual detection of ClO− in biomedical system.

On the whole, the long wavelength emission N, P-CDs has been readily exploited for rapid detection of ClO−. The proposed N, P-CDs illustrate significant selectivity and sensitivity towards ClO− over other competitive interference. In addition, the resulting N, P-CDs exhibits uniform spherical morphology, favorable dispersibility and exceptional optical features in aqueous solution, which endows it immense capabilities for application in vitro bioimaging. The N, P-CDs demonstrate regularly fluorescence decay towards different concentration of ClO− in vitro bioimaging, indicating the tremendous capacity of the proposed N, P-CDs in biological system.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: his study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21874087, 21705101, 21475080), Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (No. 201901D211155).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121170.

Yuan Jiao: Analyzed the data, Resources, Writing-original draft, Writing-review & editing. Yating Meng: Investigation, Discussed the results. Yifang Gao: Formal analysis, Discussed the results. Wenjing Lu: Project administration, Supervision. Yang Liu: Software, Visualization. Xiaojuan Gong: Project administration, Supervision. Yang Liu: Supervision. Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision. Chuan Dong: Project administration, Supervision.
